PID-MCU1: Automotive MCU with external C.name: MCU connects to CAN/LIN bus and processes safety-critical messages

threats.name: Malicious CAN/LIN Message Triggers Unsafe Actuator State
threats.desc: MCUs connected to CAN/LIN buses (Renesas V850/RH850, Freescale 9S12X, etc.) process CAN frames without message authentication; after compromising a legitimate ECU, an attacker can inject forged CAN messages to seize control of steering (EPS), braking (ABS), or powertrain (PCM). Demonstrated by Miller & Valasek 2015 via the Jeep Cherokee V850 path.

threats.name: Malicious Firmware Flash via Debug or Diagnostic Interface
threats.desc: Exploitation of the Jeep Cherokee IOC/V850 update path: malicious firmware with arbitrary CAN transmission capability injected through the SPI/firmware flash path from the Uconnect head unit to the V850 MCU. Insufficient authentication on debug ports (JTAG/SWD) presents the same threat.

threats.desc: MCUs lacking secure boot (Renesas V850/RH850, Freescale 9S12X, SH2A, etc.) load firmware without signature verification; an attacker can permanently install malicious firmware that performs CAN network attacks on every boot.

MCU (Microcontroller)._note: MCU (Microcontroller Unit): integrates a CPU core, RAM, Flash, and peripherals (CAN/SPI/UART etc.) on a single chip for embedded control. Unlike an MPU, an MCU can operate standalone without external memory chips. Optimized for real-time control (RTOS or bare-metal). Common automotive ECU MCUs: Renesas V850 (Jeep IOC), RH850 (EPS), Freescale 9S12X (BMW TCB), SH2A (Mercedes HERMES), Infineon TriCore, NXP S32K series.

cwes.name: Cleartext Transmission of Sensitive Information

SoC (System-on-Chip)._note: SoC (System-on-Chip): integrates MPU (CPU), GPU, DSP, communication modem, and other complex subsystems on a single chip. Far higher integration than an MCU. Used in IVI infotainment, telematics, and gateway platforms. Automotive examples: OMAP-DM3730 (Jeep Uconnect), NVIDIA Tegra 3 (Tesla CID/IC), Renesas R-Car H2 (Mercedes NTG5.5), Qualcomm MDM6200 (BMW TCB). Key distinction from MCU: SoC runs a full OS (Linux/QNX/Ubuntu) for high-performance tasks; MCU runs real-time firmware (RTOS/bare-metal) for low-power control.

PID-ESIM1: Device includes embedded SIM.name: Device contains eSIM with OTA remote SIM provisioning capability

threats.name: Unauthorized Remote eSIM Profile Manipulation
threats.desc: Authentication vulnerability in the eSIM RSP (Remote SIM Provisioning) channel; an unauthorized SM-DP+ server can inject a malicious profile into the vehicle eSIM or delete the legitimate profile, causing cellular blackout (DoS) or creating an eavesdropping path. The Mercedes-Benz HERMES TCU eSIM is a representative case.

eSIM._note: Embedded SIM soldered into the Mercedes-Benz HERMES TCU. Includes threats related to Remote SIM Provisioning (RSP) security.

cwes.name: Protection Mechanism Failure
cwes.desc: CAN transceivers provide no message authentication at the physical layer; without MAC/authentication implemented in upper layers (software), spoofing is unchecked.
cwes.description: Security protection mechanism missing or bypassable.

cwes.name: Authentication Bypass by Spoofing
cwes.desc: CAN transceivers do not verify message origin; any node with bus access can inject arbitrary CAN messages.
cwes.description: Attacker spoofs legitimate entity to bypass authentication.

cwes.name: Uncontrolled Resource Consumption
cwes.desc: Bus flooding via repeated CAN error frames triggers Bus-Off state on the CAN transceiver, causing complete communication loss for the target ECU (DoS).
cwes.description: Uncontrolled resource consumption enables denial of service.

PID-CANT1: Device uses CAN transceiver f.name: Device includes CAN transceiver connected to vehicle CAN bus (no MAC/authentication)

threats.name: Unauthenticated CAN Message Injection via Transceiver
threats.desc: ISO 11898 CAN transceivers provide no message authentication; an attacker with bus access (via a compromised ECU or physical connection) can inject arbitrary CAN ID and data frames. No defense without CAN-FD or AUTOSAR SecOC.

cwes.name: Authentication Bypass by Spoofing
cwes.desc: The product does not properly authenticate messages from another entity.

cwes.name: Insufficient Verification of Data Authenticity
cwes.desc: The product does not sufficiently verify the authenticity of data.

cwes.name: Protection Mechanism Failure
cwes.desc: The product does not use a protection mechanism against directed attacks.

threats.name: CAN Bus-Off Attack (DoS via Error Frame Flooding)
threats.desc: Repeatedly injecting CAN error frames drives the target ECU's transmit error counter (TEC) to maximum, forcing it into Bus-Off state; in environments without AUTOSAR SecOC, safety-critical ECUs (EPS, ABS) lose CAN communication entirely.

cwes.name: Uncontrolled Resource Consumption
cwes.desc: The product does not control resource allocation, allowing DoS.

cwes.name: Improper Check or Handling of Exceptional Conditions
cwes.desc: The product does not properly handle error conditions.

cwes.name: Authentication Bypass by Spoofing
cwes.desc: The product does not properly authenticate messages from another entity.

cwes.name: Insufficient Verification of Data Authenticity
cwes.desc: The product does not sufficiently verify the authenticity of data.

cwes.name: Protection Mechanism Failure
cwes.desc: The product does not use a protection mechanism against directed attacks.

cwes.name: Uncontrolled Resource Consumption
cwes.desc: The product does not control resource allocation, allowing DoS.

cwes.name: Improper Check or Handling of Exceptional Conditions
cwes.desc: The product does not properly handle error conditions.

CAN Transceiver._note: CAN transceiver implementing ISO 11898 physical/data-link layer (e.g., NXP TJA1040/1050, Microchip MCP2551). Present in Mercedes-Benz HERMES TCU and each Jeep Cherokee ECU. Absence of message authentication is the fundamental vulnerability enabling vehicle hacking.

cwes.name: Authentication Bypass by Spoofing
cwes.desc: ABS/ESP/EPS ECUs do not authenticate CAN message senders and can receive manipulated control commands from other compromised ECUs.
cwes.description: Attacker spoofs legitimate entity to bypass authentication.

cwes.name: Improper Access Control
cwes.desc: Insufficient CAN message access control on safety ECUs; any node on the broadcast bus can send safety-critical commands.
cwes.description: Access control mechanisms not properly enforced; unauthorized actions possible.

cwes.name: Insecure Default Initialization of Resource
cwes.desc: Safety ECU accepts CAN messages in a default-trusted state during initialization; attacker can inject malicious commands during this initialization phase.

cwes.name: Improper Check for Unusual or Exceptional Conditions
cwes.desc: ABS/ESP ECU executes out-of-range or abnormally-timed CAN messages without exception handling, causing unsafe states.

PID-SAFE1: Safety ECU receives CAN comma.name: Safety-critical ECU (ABS, ESP, EPS, PCM) processes CAN messages without MAC/authentication

threats.name: Safety Function Hijacking via Injected CAN Command
threats.desc: Safety-critical ECUs (ABS/ESP/EPS/PCM) execute commands received on the CAN bus without authenticating the sender; forged CAN frames from a compromised IVI, gateway, or TCU enable takeover of braking, steering, and engine control. Demonstrated in Jeep Cherokee (V850 → CAN-C → EPS/ABS/PCM) and Tesla (IC → GW → CHASSIS).

cwes.name: Authentication Bypass by Spoofing
cwes.desc: The product does not properly authenticate messages from another entity.

cwes.name: Improper Access Control
cwes.desc: The product does not restrict or incorrectly restricts access.

cwes.name: Insufficient Verification of Data Authenticity
cwes.desc: The product does not sufficiently verify the authenticity of data.

threats.name: Safety ECU Diagnostic Exploitation via UDS
threats.desc: Entering a UDS diagnostic session on a Safety ECU allows ECU reset, parameter modification, and firmware upload; BMW 2019 demo showed downstream Safety ECU UDS access via Central Gateway.

cwes.name: Missing Authentication for Critical Function
cwes.desc: The product does not perform authentication for critical functionality.

cwes.name: Improper Authorization
cwes.desc: The product does not perform an authorization check.

cwes.name: Authentication Bypass by Spoofing
cwes.desc: The product does not properly authenticate messages from another entity.

cwes.name: Improper Access Control
cwes.desc: The product does not restrict or incorrectly restricts access.

cwes.name: Insufficient Verification of Data Authenticity
cwes.desc: The product does not sufficiently verify the authenticity of data.

cwes.name: Missing Authentication for Critical Function
cwes.desc: The product does not perform authentication for critical functionality.

cwes.desc: The product does not perform an authorization check.

Safety ECU (ABS/ESP/EPS)._note: Safety-critical ECUs: Bosch ESP/ABS module (Jeep Cherokee), Renesas RH850-based EPS ECU (Jeep Cherokee), Denso PCM (Jeep Cherokee), Tesla CHASSIS domain ECUs, BMW chassis/body domain ECUs. Absence of CAN message authentication is the core vulnerability.

cwes.name: Authentication Bypass by Spoofing
cwes.desc: LIN bus uses a single-master architecture but does not support message authentication; master compromise allows control of all slaves (doors, windows, lights, etc.).
cwes.description: Attacker spoofs legitimate entity to bypass authentication.

cwes.name: Insufficient Verification of Data Authenticity
cwes.desc: LIN frames lack sender authentication and integrity protection.
cwes.description: Data authenticity not sufficiently verified; spoofed messages accepted.

cwes.name: Uncontrolled Resource Consumption
cwes.desc: LIN bus flooding causes slave response loss (DoS).
cwes.description: Uncontrolled resource consumption enables denial of service.

PID-LIN1: Device uses LIN bus for body f.name: Device includes LIN transceiver/master connected to body-domain slave ECUs

threats.name: LIN Master Compromise Enables Body Function Hijacking
threats.desc: When the LIN master (e.g., Mercedes-Benz HERMES TCU LIN interface) is compromised, all connected LIN slaves (door locks, windows, lights, seat control) can be commanded without authorization; the LIN protocol itself provides no slave-side authentication mechanism.

cwes.name: Authentication Bypass by Spoofing
cwes.desc: LIN slaves accept commands from any node claiming to be master.

cwes.name: Improper Access Control
cwes.desc: No access control on LIN bus frame reception.

cwes.name: Authentication Bypass by Spoofing
cwes.desc: LIN slaves accept commands from any node claiming to be master.

cwes.name: Improper Access Control
cwes.desc: No access control on LIN bus frame reception.

cwes.desc: The chip does not implement or does not correctly perform access control to check whether users are authorized to access internal registers and test modes through the physical debug/test interface.

threats.desc: If a device has a latent user access port, it may be possible for attackers to leverage physical access to obtain privileges that were not accounted for when considering software or remote access cont

cwes.name: Missing Protection Mechanism for Alternate Hardware Interface
cwes.desc: The lack of protections on alternate paths to access control-protected assets (such as unprotected shadow registers and other external facing unguarded interfaces) allows an attacker to bypass existing protections to the asset that are only performed against the primary path.

cwes.name: On-Chip Debug and Test Interface With Improper Access Control
cwes.desc: The chip does not implement or does not correctly perform access control to check whether users are authorized to access internal registers and test modes through the physical debug/test interface.

threats.name: Latent Hardware Debug Port Allows Memory/Code Manipulation
threats.desc: Hardware debugging ports (e.g., JTAG, SWD, etc.) oftentimes have high privileges or direct access to the running device's memory and integrated hardware. By leveraging one of these hardware debugging

cwes.name: On-Chip Debug and Test Interface With Improper Access Control
cwes.desc: The chip does not implement or does not correctly perform access control to check whether users are authorized to access internal registers and test modes through the physical debug/test interface.

cwes.name: Missing Protection Mechanism for Alternate Hardware Interface
cwes.desc: The lack of protections on alternate paths to access control-protected assets (such as unprotected shadow registers and other external facing unguarded interfaces) allows an attacker to bypass existing protections to the asset that are only performed against the primary path.

cwes.name: On-Chip Debug and Test Interface With Improper Access Control
cwes.desc: The chip does not implement or does not correctly perform access control to check whether users are authorized to access internal registers and test modes through the physical debug/test interface.

Test / Debug HW._note: Test and debug hardware (logic analyzers, in-circuit emulators, pin headers) shares the same threat model as Debug/Access Port (physical access → firmware dump/tampering) rather than Peripheral/Bus. Correctly categorized under Debug/Access Port.

cwes.name: Improper Preservation of Consistency Between Independent Representations of Shared State
cwes.desc: The product has or supports multiple distributed components or sub-systems that are each required to keep their own local copy of shared data - such as state or cache - but the product does not ensure

cwes.name: Missing Immutable Root of Trust in Hardware
cwes.desc: A missing immutable root of trust in the hardware results in the ability to bypass secure boot or execute untrusted or adversarial boot code.

cwes.name: Security Version Number Mutable to Older Versions
cwes.desc: Security-version number in hardware is mutable, resulting in the ability to downgrade (roll-back) the boot firmware to vulnerable code versions.

cwes.name: Remanent Data Readable after Memory Erase
cwes.desc: Confidential information stored in memory circuits is readable or recoverable after being cleared or erased.

cwes.name: Improper Isolation of Shared Resources in Network On Chip (NoC)
cwes.desc: The Network On Chip (NoC) does not isolate or incorrectly isolates its on-chip-fabric and internal resources such that they are shared between trusted and untrusted agents, creating timing channels.

cwes.name: Missing Security-Relevant Feedback for Unexecuted Operations in Hardware Interface
cwes.desc: The product has a hardware interface that silently discards operations in situations for which feedback would be security-relevant, such as the timely detection of failures or attacks.

Security HW._note: HSM/TPM/Secure Element/PUF: provides cryptographic operations, key storage, and hardware root of trust. Fundamentally different function and threat model from Debug/Access Port. Placed in the dedicated Hardware > Security Module category.

cwes.name: Information Exposure through Microarchitectural State after Transient Execution
cwes.desc: The processor does not properly clear microarchitectural state after incorrect microcode assists or speculative execution, resulting in transient execution.

cwes.name: Missing Security-Relevant Feedback for Unexecuted Operations in Hardware Interface
cwes.desc: The product has a hardware interface that silently discards operations in situations for which feedback would be security-relevant, such as the timely detection of failures or attacks.

x86 / x64 Architecture._note: x86/x64 architecture (IA-32, AMD64): ISA used by Intel and AMD CPUs. Applied in automotive x86-based infotainment (e.g., BMW NBT HU-Intel). This kind classifies the CPU architecture (ISA), not a processor product type. Microarchitectural side-channel CWEs such as Spectre (CVE-2017-5753) and Meltdown (CVE-2017-5754) are specific to this architecture.

cwes.name: Information Exposure through Microarchitectural State after Transient Execution
cwes.desc: The processor does not properly clear microarchitectural state after incorrect microcode assists or speculative execution, resulting in transient execution.

cwes.name: Missing Security-Relevant Feedback for Unexecuted Operations in Hardware Interface
cwes.desc: The product has a hardware interface that silently discards operations in situations for which feedback would be security-relevant, such as the timely detection of failures or attacks.

ARM Architecture._note: ARM architecture (ARMv7, ARMv8/AArch64): low-power, high-efficiency RISC ISA. Used in the vast majority of automotive SoCs, MCUs, and ECUs (Cortex-A: IVI SoC; Cortex-M: MCU; Cortex-R: RTOS ECU). This kind classifies the CPU architecture (ISA), not a processor product type. Covers ARM-specific vulnerabilities including TrustZone bypass and cache side-channels (CVE-2017-5715).

cwes.name: Sensitive Data Storage in Improperly Locked Memory
cwes.desc: The product stores sensitive data in memory that is not locked, or that has been incorrectly locked, which might cause the memory to be written to swap files on disk by the virtual memory manager. Thi
cwes.description: Sensitive data stored in memory region accessible to other processes.

cwes.name: Concurrent Execution using Shared Resource with Improper Synchronization ('Race Condition')
cwes.desc: Linux kernel TOCTOU / UAF race conditions; the most frequent vulnerability class in kernel CVEs.
cwes.description: Race condition in concurrent code leads to unexpected state transitions.

cves.description: Dirty COW: Linux kernel mm/gup.c race condition in copy-on-write. Local priv esc to root. Affects all Linux 2.6.22–4.8.3. Relevant: Tesla CID/IC (Ubuntu) privilege escalation vector.

cwes.name: Use After Free
cwes.desc: Attacks reusing freed kernel objects; a primary privilege escalation path (includes Dirty COW-class exploits).
cwes.description: Memory use after it has been freed leads to crash or code execution.

cves.description: Linux kernel futex() requeue use-after-free. Local priv esc; affects kernels before 3.14.5. Notable in embedded Linux-based automotive systems.
Linux kernel net/xfrm/xfrm_user.c UAF via XFRM_MSG_NEWSA command. Local priv esc. Affects kernels before 4.13.11.

cwes.name: Out-of-bounds Write
cwes.desc: Buffer overflow in netfilter/BPF/perf subsystems; leads to kernel heap corruption.
cwes.description: Write operation beyond allocated buffer; memory corruption / code execution.

cwes.name: Out-of-bounds Read
cwes.desc: Out-of-bounds reads in kernel code paths; leaks sensitive memory such as pointers and credentials.
cwes.description: Read beyond allocated buffer boundary; information disclosure or crash.

cwes.name: Improper Privilege Management
cwes.desc: Abuse of Linux capabilities (CAP_*) or SUID bits; includes container escape scenarios.

cwes.name: Improper Link Resolution Before File Access ('Link Following')
cwes.desc: Root-privileged daemon follows symbolic or hard links into user-controlled paths, allowing file overwrites.

cwes.name: Use of Externally-Controlled Format String
cwes.desc: Format string attacks against syslog/logging daemons; allows arbitrary code execution in user-space processes.

cwes.name: Integer Overflow or Wraparound
cwes.desc: Integer overflow in kmalloc/vmalloc path leads to heap corruption and privilege escalation.
cwes.description: Integer arithmetic overflow wraps around, causing unexpected behavior.

threats.name: Malicious LKM-based Rootkit Installation
threats.desc: An attacker loads an unsigned LKM via a vulnerable kernel interface to install a rootkit; kernel function hooking can hide processes, files, and network traffic.

cwes.desc: The product does not use or incorrectly uses a protection mechanism.

Linux._note: Inherits EMB3D PID-23 series properties (rootkit, network stack, privilege escalation, memory management threats). Linux-specific properties: LKM rootkit (PID-23L1), namespace escape (PID-23L2), capability/SUID abuse (PID-23L3), D-Bus exposure (PID-23L4), kernel network stack exploits (PID-23L5). CWE coverage expanded from 1 (CWE-591) in ver0.1 to 9 entries.

cwes.name: Concurrent Execution using Shared Resource with Improper Synchronization ('Race Condition')
cwes.desc: Race condition in QNX IPC channels or shared memory regions; can violate real-time safety guarantees.
cwes.description: Race condition in concurrent code leads to unexpected state transitions.

cwes.name: Improper Locking
cwes.desc: Mutex/semaphore misuse in RTOS environment causes priority inversion and deadline misses, resulting in safety service DoS or privilege escalation.
cwes.description: Improper locking of shared resources leads to race conditions.

cwes.name: Uncontrolled Resource Consumption
cwes.desc: Exhaustion of QNX pulse queue, message channel, or timer resources causes safety service DoS.
cwes.description: Uncontrolled resource consumption enables denial of service.

cves.description: QNX SDP io-pkt (network stack) heap overflow on malformed packet processing. Remote DoS / potential RCE on networked QNX devices. Relevant to any QNX device with network stack exposed (TCU/IVI).

cwes.name: NULL Pointer Dereference
cwes.desc: NULL dereference in a QNX resource manager or driver crashes safety-related services.
cwes.description: NULL pointer dereference causes application crash.

cwes.name: Integer Overflow or Wraparound
cwes.desc: Integer overflow in QNX driver or IPC buffer size calculation leads to heap corruption.
cwes.description: Integer arithmetic overflow wraps around, causing unexpected behavior.

cves.description: QNX SDP (Neutrino RTOS) calloc() integer overflow in C runtime library. Heap buffer overflow → RCE possible. Disclosed 2021 (Armis/BlackBerry). Affects QNX 6.x/7.x; relevant to Jeep 2015 (QNX TCU) and BMW 2019 (QNX TCU).

cwes.name: Improper Input Validation
cwes.desc: QNX resource manager insufficiently validates message payloads from untrusted clients.
cwes.description: Improper validation of input data allows attacker to inject malformed data.

cwes.name: Improper Link Resolution Before File Access ('Link Following')
cwes.desc: Symbolic link following vulnerability in QNX POSIX-compatible filesystem; exploits privileged resource managers.

cwes.name: External Control of File Name or Path
cwes.desc: Unvalidated path input in QNX resource manager allows directory traversal.

PID-23Q1: QNX uses microkernel IPC messa.name: Device OS is QNX and uses microkernel IPC (MsgSend/MsgReceive) between processes

threats.name: Malicious IPC Message Triggers Resource Manager Exploit
threats.desc: The QNX microkernel relies on IPC message passing between processes; a malicious client can trigger a buffer overflow or type confusion in a resource manager via crafted messages, achieving code execution with resource manager privileges (root or driver-level).

QNX._note: QNX Neutrino RTOS: POSIX-compatible microkernel real-time OS. Used in Jeep Cherokee Uconnect (QNX 6.5, Miller & Valasek 2015) and BMW NBT head unit. Inherits EMB3D PID-23 series properties. QNX-specific threats: IPC message manipulation (PID-23Q1), RTOS priority inversion DoS (PID-23Q2), resource manager hijacking (PID-23Q3), remote network service exploitation (PID-23Q4).

cwes.name: Improper Limitation of a Pathname to a Restricted Directory ('Path Traversal')
cwes.desc: Ubuntu apt/dpkg or snap package extraction and installation path traversal; malicious packages can overwrite system files.

cwes.name: Missing Authorization
cwes.desc: Authorization bypass in Ubuntu D-Bus services and polkit configuration; includes CVE-2021-3560 (polkit 15-second race condition).
cwes.description: Authorization check missing; operation proceeds without verification.

cwes.name: Improper Privilege Management
cwes.desc: sudo misconfiguration or SUID binary abuse for local privilege escalation; a Tesla IVI root escape path.

cwes.name: Incorrect Permission Assignment for Critical Resource
cwes.desc: Ubuntu snap confinement escape or missing AppArmor profile exposes sensitive vehicle data.
cwes.description: Incorrect permissions on critical resources allow unauthorized access.

cwes.name: Download of Code Without Integrity Check
cwes.desc: Missing package signature verification or MITM during OTA apt-get update allows malicious package installation.

cves.description: Dirty COW: Linux kernel race condition in mm/gup.c. Directly affects Ubuntu 14.04/16.04 LTS — Tesla CID (Ubuntu 14.04) used these kernels. Exploited in Tesla Model S/X privilege escalation (Keen Security Lab 2016/2017).
Linux futex() UAF; exploitable on Ubuntu LTS kernels before patch. Tesla IVI Ubuntu-based systems potentially affected.

PID-23U1: Ubuntu snap or AppArmor confin.name: Device uses Ubuntu snap packages or AppArmor mandatory access control

threats.name: Snap Confinement Escape via Kernel or snapd Vulnerability
threats.desc: snap confinement or AppArmor profile bypass; snapd vulnerabilities (e.g., CVE-2019-7304 dirty_sock) allow a confined app to access the host filesystem and execute root commands. Applicable to the Tesla Tegra-based Ubuntu platform isolation escape scenario.

cwes.name: Incorrect Permission Assignment for Critical Resource
cwes.desc: The product specifies permissions for a security-critical resource in a way that allows access.

cwes.name: Improper Privilege Management
cwes.desc: The product does not properly assign, modify, track, or check privileges.

cwes.name: Incorrect Permission Assignment for Critical Resource
cwes.desc: The product specifies permissions for a security-critical resource in a way that allows access.

cwes.desc: The product does not properly assign, modify, track, or check privileges.

PID-23U2: Ubuntu D-Bus with polkit autho.name: Device exposes D-Bus services controlled by polkit for privilege separation

threats.name: polkit Authorization Bypass (Race Condition or Logic Flaw)
threats.desc: Race condition in polkit authorization flow (CVE-2021-3560: 15-second timing) or pkexec path handling error (CVE-2021-4034: PwnKit) allows an unprivileged local user to execute root-level commands. Applies to the local privilege escalation step in the Tesla FREE-FALL chain after pivoting from CID to IC.

Ubuntu._note: Ubuntu: Debian-based Linux distribution. Used in Tesla Model S/X CID and IC (Tegra-based; FREE-FALL 2017 / OTA 2018). Inherits Linux CWEs and EMB3D PID-23 series properties. Ubuntu-specific threats: snap/AppArmor escape (PID-23U1), polkit authorization bypass (PID-23U2), apt OTA package integrity (PID-23U3), network service exposure (PID-23U4).

cwes.name: Untrusted Search Path
cwes.desc: Windows Automotive DLL search path manipulation; loads malicious DLL from vehicle-side storage (DLL hijacking).

cwes.name: Download of Code Without Integrity Check
cwes.desc: Insufficient cryptographic integrity verification during OTA or USB software update allows malicious firmware installation.

cwes.name: Insufficient Verification of Data Authenticity
cwes.desc: Insufficient authentication of remote service commands and OTA payloads (e.g., Mercedes-Benz NTG 5.5 WCC protocol).
cwes.description: Data authenticity not sufficiently verified; spoofed messages accepted.

cwes.name: External Control of File Name or Path
cwes.desc: Path traversal vulnerability in Windows path handling (UNC paths, 8.3 names, trailing backslashes).

cwes.name: Improper Certificate Validation
cwes.desc: Insufficient TLS or code-signing certificate validation in Windows Automotive; enables MITM or rogue server attacks.
cwes.description: TLS certificate not properly validated; MitM attack possible.

threats.name: Unsigned Software/Firmware Installation via OTA or USB
threats.desc: Unsigned software installable via USB or OTA on Windows Automotive head units (e.g., Mercedes-Benz NTG 5.5); bypassing code-signing validation loads malicious infotainment software that can access CAN control paths.

Windows Automotive._note: Windows Automotive (Windows Automotive 7): used in Mercedes-Benz NTG 5.5 head unit (Sky-Go / Black Hat 2020, Yan et al.). Windows Embedded family; inherits Windows CWEs and EMB3D PID-23 series. Additional threats: OTA/USB unsigned software installation (PID-23W1), remote service exploit (PID-23W2), DLL hijacking (PID-23W3).

cwes.name: Improper Input Validation
cwes.desc: Insufficient input validation in AT command parsing or SMS/network protocol handling leads to code execution on the modem side.
cwes.description: Improper validation of input data allows attacker to inject malformed data.

cwes.name: Heap-based Buffer Overflow
cwes.desc: Heap overflow in Qualcomm baseband protocol parsers (GSM, LTE RRC/NAS); historically significant remote attack surface in Qualcomm modem CVE class.

cwes.name: Out-of-bounds Write
cwes.desc: OOB Write in modem stack message handler leads to baseband code execution and AP isolation bypass.
cwes.description: Write operation beyond allocated buffer; memory corruption / code execution.

cwes.name: Out-of-bounds Read
cwes.desc: Protocol decoder OOB Read leaks modem memory contents across the AP boundary.
cwes.description: Read beyond allocated buffer boundary; information disclosure or crash.

cwes.name: Integer Overflow or Wraparound
cwes.desc: Integer overflow in AMSS memory allocation or protocol length fields leads to heap corruption.
cwes.description: Integer arithmetic overflow wraps around, causing unexpected behavior.

cwes.name: Improper Access Control
cwes.desc: Insufficient access control between AMSS/REX OS modem tasks; a compromised task can directly access CAN-connected MCUs via USB/SPI bridge.
cwes.description: Access control mechanisms not properly enforced; unauthorized actions possible.

cwes.name: Missing Authentication for Critical Function
cwes.desc: Missing authentication for critical modem functions on the AT command interface.
cwes.description: Critical functionality accessible without requiring authentication.

PID-23A1: AMSS baseband protocol parser.name: Device includes AMSS/REX-based baseband processor that parses cellular network protocols

threats.name: Remote Code Execution via Malformed Baseband Protocol Message
threats.desc: Injecting crafted messages into cellular protocols (GSM/LTE RRC, NAS, SMS) processed by the AMSS baseband; known Qualcomm modem heap overflow vulnerabilities (e.g., CVE-2020-3654) enable remote code execution at the baseband level. BMW TCB MDM6200 is exposed via cellular network.

cwes.name: Heap-based Buffer Overflow
cwes.desc: A heap-based buffer overflow occurs in the memory allocated via malloc.

cwes.name: Out-of-bounds Write
cwes.desc: The product writes data past the end of the intended buffer.

cwes.desc: The product does not validate or incorrectly validates input.

AMSS/REX OS._note: AMSS/REX OS: proprietary real-time OS running on the Qualcomm MDM6200 modem processor. Used in BMW TCB (Telematics Communication Box), directly exposed to cellular networks (Black Hat 2019). Inherits EMB3D PID-23 series properties. AMSS-specific threats: baseband protocol RCE (PID-23A1), AT command injection (PID-23A2), modem-to-AP privilege escalation (PID-23A3), rogue base station remote attack (PID-23A4).

cwes.name: Storage of Sensitive Data in a Mechanism without Access Control

cwes.name: Improper Verification of Intent by Broadcast Receiver
cwes.desc: The Android application uses a Broadcast Receiver that receives an Intent but does not properly verify that the Intent came from an authorized source.

cwes.name: Improper Export of Android Application Components
cwes.desc: The Android application exports a component for use by other applications, but does not properly restrict which applications can launch the component or access the data it contains.

cwes.name: Use of Implicit Intent for Sensitive Communication
cwes.desc: The Android application uses an implicit intent for transmitting sensitive data to other applications.

cwes.name: Incorrectly Specified Destination in a Communication Channel
cwes.desc: The product creates a communication channel to initiate an outgoing request to an actor, but it does not correctly specify the intended destination for that actor.

cves.description: Android Binder kernel driver use-after-free (binder_poll). Exploited by NSO Group Pegasus; local priv esc to kernel. Relevant to BMW/Mercedes Android companion apps and Android Automotive OS.

Android 'Janus' vulnerability: APK signature bypass via prepended DEX file. Allows replacement of legitimate app (e.g., vehicle OEM companion app) with malicious version while preserving valid signature. Affects Android 5.x–8.x.

cwes.name: Improper Authorization in Handler for Custom URL Scheme
cwes.desc: The product uses a handler for a custom URL scheme, but it does not properly restrict which actors can invoke the handler using the scheme.

cves.description: Apple iOS/macOS kernel UAF in BSD socket layer. Local privilege escalation to kernel. Relevant to Tesla/BMW companion app iOS attack surface.
iOS TCC bypass via environment variable injection (SQLITE_AUTO_VACUUM trick). Allows unauthorized access to private data from vehicle companion apps.

Telematics Software._note: Automotive telematics software stack. Applicable cases: Mercedes HERMES TCU (ATP SMS handling), BMW ConnectedDrive (NGTP), Tesla cid-updater/ic-updater (OTA). Key threats: remote backend compromise (PID-TEL1) and proprietary protocol bypass (PID-TEL2).

cwes.name: Missing Authentication for Critical Function

cwes.description: Access control mechanisms not properly enforced; unauthorized actions possible.

cwes.description: Improper validation of input data allows attacker to inject malformed data.

Automotive Middleware._note: Vehicle middleware based on AUTOSAR, SOME/IP, or DDS. Security threats in service-oriented architecture (SOA) contexts.

cwes.name: Use After Free
cwes.desc: Use-after-free on DOM objects in WebKit/QtWebKit rendering engine; malicious web content achieves arbitrary code execution in the renderer process. Initial entry point in Tesla FREE-FALL (2017) (CVE-2016-4657).
cwes.description: Memory use after it has been freed leads to crash or code execution.

cves.description: WebKit JSC (JavaScriptCore) ArrayPrototype::fastSlice type confusion → UAF. Exploited by Keen Security Lab to achieve RCE in Tesla Model S browser (2016 report). Fixed in Safari 9.1.2 / iOS 9.3.3; automotive WebKit versions often unpatched for months.
WebKit UAF in WebCore processing. Remote code execution via malicious web page. Fixed in iOS 13.1/Safari 13. Automotive browsers based on WebKit 605.x or earlier affected.

cwes.name: Out-of-bounds Write
cwes.desc: OOB Write in JavaScript engine (JSC/V8) or HTML parser gives control of the renderer process.
cwes.description: Write operation beyond allocated buffer; memory corruption / code execution.

cwes.name: Improper Neutralization of Input During Web Page Generation ('Cross-site Scripting')

cwes.description: XSS: Attacker injects scripts that execute in victim's browser context.

cves.description: WebKit universal XSS via crafted web content. Allows attacker to steal cookies and execute scripts in any origin context. Relevant for IVI browsers loading remote content (e.g., streaming services, OEM portals).

cwes.name: Improper Restriction of Operations within the Bounds of a Memory Buffer
cwes.desc: Memory corruption vulnerability in WebKit media, font, or image parsers.
cwes.description: Buffer operations without bounds checking allow memory corruption.

cwes.desc: The web application improperly neutralizes user-controlled input for executable script disguised with URI encodings.

cwes.desc: The product receives data from an HTTP agent/component (e.g., web server, proxy, browser, etc.), but it does not neutralize or incorrectly neutralizes CR and LF characters before the data is included

cwes.name: Improper Encoding or Escaping of Output
cwes.desc: The product prepares a structured message for communication with another component, but encoding or escaping of the data is either missing or done incorrectly. As a result, the intended structure of t

cwes.name: Storage of File with Sensitive Data Under Web Root
cwes.desc: The product stores sensitive data under the web document root with insufficient access control, which might make it accessible to untrusted parties.

cwes.name: J2EE Bad Practices: Direct Management of Connections
cwes.desc: The J2EE application directly manages connections, instead of using the container's connection management facilities.

cwes.name: Improper Access Control
cwes.desc: The product does not restrict or incorrectly restricts access to a resource from an unauthorized actor.
cwes.description: Access control mechanisms not properly enforced; unauthorized actions possible.

cwes.name: Improper Authorization
cwes.desc: The product does not perform or incorrectly performs an authorization check when an actor attempts to access a resource or perform an action.
cwes.description: Authorization check absent or incorrectly implemented.

cwes.name: Improper Authentication
cwes.desc: When an actor claims to have a given identity, the product does not prove or insufficiently proves that the claim is correct.
cwes.description: Authentication mechanisms are weak, missing, or bypassable.

cwes.name: Authentication Bypass Using an Alternate Path or Channel
cwes.desc: The product requires authentication, but the product has an alternate path or channel that does not require authentication.

cwes.name: Improper Certificate Validation
cwes.desc: The product does not validate, or incorrectly validates, a certificate.
cwes.description: TLS certificate not properly validated; MitM attack possible.

cwes.name: Improper Following of a Certificate's Chain of Trust
cwes.desc: The product does not follow, or incorrectly follows, the chain of trust for a certificate back to a trusted root certificate, resulting in incorrect trust of any resource that is associated with that

cwes.name: Improper Validation of Certificate with Host Mismatch
cwes.desc: The product communicates with a host that provides a certificate, but the product does not properly ensure that the certificate is actually associated with that host.

cwes.name: Improper Check for Certificate Revocation
cwes.desc: The product does not check or incorrectly checks the revocation status of a certificate, which may cause it to use a certificate that has been compromised.

cwes.name: Authentication Bypass by Assumed-Immutable Data
cwes.desc: The authentication scheme or implementation uses key data elements that are assumed to be immutable, but can be controlled or modified by the attacker.

cwes.name: Origin Validation Error
cwes.desc: The product does not properly verify that the source of data or communication is valid.
cwes.description: Message origin not validated; forged messages accepted as legitimate.

cwes.name: Cross-Site Request Forgery (CSRF)
cwes.desc: The web application does not, or cannot, sufficiently verify whether a request was intentionally provided by the user who sent the request, which could have originated from an unauthorized actor.

cwes.name: J2EE Bad Practices: Use of System.exit()
cwes.desc: A J2EE application uses System.exit(), which also shuts down its container.

cwes.name: J2EE Bad Practices: Direct Use of Threads
cwes.desc: Thread management in a Web application is forbidden in some circumstances and is always highly error prone.

cwes.name: Session Fixation
cwes.desc: Authenticating a user, or otherwise establishing a new user session, without invalidating any existing session identifier gives an attacker the opportunity to steal authenticated sessions.

cwes.name: Improper Protection of Alternate Path
cwes.desc: The product does not sufficiently protect all possible paths that a user can take to access restricted functionality or resources.

cwes.name: Direct Request ('Forced Browsing')
cwes.desc: The web application does not adequately enforce appropriate authorization on all restricted URLs, scripts, or files.

cwes.name: Unparsed Raw Web Content Delivery
cwes.desc: The product stores raw content or supporting code under the web document root with an extension that is not specifically handled by the server.

cwes.name: Unrestricted Upload of File with Dangerous Type
cwes.desc: The product allows the upload or transfer of dangerous file types that are automatically processed within its environment.

cwes.name: Inconsistent Interpretation of HTTP Requests ('HTTP Request/Response Smuggling')
cwes.desc: The product acts as an intermediary HTTP agent (such as a proxy or firewall) in the data flow between two entities such as a client and server, but it does not interpret malformed HTTP requests or res

cwes.name: Modification of Assumed-Immutable Data (MAID)
cwes.desc: The product does not properly protect an assumed-immutable element from being modified by an attacker.

cwes.name: External Control of Assumed-Immutable Web Parameter
cwes.desc: The web application does not sufficiently verify inputs that are assumed to be immutable but are actually externally controllable, such as hidden form fields.

cwes.name: Exposure of Sensitive System Information to an Unauthorized Control Sphere
cwes.desc: The product does not properly prevent sensitive system-level information from being accessed by unauthorized actors who do not have the same level of access to the underlying system as the product doe

cwes.name: .NET Misconfiguration: Use of Impersonation
cwes.desc: Allowing a .NET application to run at potentially escalated levels of access to the underlying operating and file systems can be dangerous and result in various forms of attacks.

cwes.name: Insufficiently Protected Credentials
cwes.desc: The product transmits or stores authentication credentials, but it uses an insecure method that is susceptible to unauthorized interception and/or retrieval.

cwes.name: Use of Web Browser Cache Containing Sensitive Information
cwes.desc: The web application does not use an appropriate caching policy that specifies the extent to which each web page and associated form fields should be cached.

cwes.name: Exposure of Version-Control Repository to an Unauthorized Control Sphere
cwes.desc: The product stores a CVS, git, or other repository in a directory, archive, or other resource that is stored, transferred, or otherwise made accessible to unauthorized actors.

cwes.name: Exposure of Backup File to an Unauthorized Control Sphere
cwes.desc: A backup file is stored in a directory or archive that is made accessible to unauthorized actors.

cwes.name: Servlet Runtime Error Message Containing Sensitive Information
cwes.desc: A servlet error message indicates that there exists an unhandled exception in the web application code and may provide useful information to an attacker.

cwes.name: Use of Persistent Cookies Containing Sensitive Information
cwes.desc: The web application uses persistent cookies, but the cookies contain sensitive information.

cwes.name: Inclusion of Sensitive Information in Source Code
cwes.desc: Source code on a web server or repository often contains sensitive information and should generally not be accessible to users.

cwes.name: Missing Password Field Masking
cwes.desc: The product does not mask passwords during entry, increasing the potential for attackers to observe and capture passwords.

cwes.name: Incorrect Behavior Order: Authorization Before Parsing and Canonicalization
cwes.desc: If a web server does not fully parse requested URLs before it examines them for authorization, it may be possible for an attacker to bypass authorization protection.

cwes.name: Command Shell in Externally Accessible Directory
cwes.desc: A possible shell file exists in /cgi-bin/ or other accessible directories. This is extremely dangerous and can be used by an attacker to execute commands on the web server.

cwes.name: J2EE Framework: Saving Unserializable Objects to Disk
cwes.desc: When the J2EE container attempts to write unserializable objects to disk there is no guarantee that the process will complete successfully.

cwes.name: Use of GET Request Method With Sensitive Query Strings
cwes.desc: The web application uses the HTTP GET method to process a request and includes sensitive information in the query string of that request.

cwes.name: Uncaught Exception in Servlet
cwes.desc: The Servlet does not catch all exceptions, which may reveal sensitive debugging information.

cwes.name: URL Redirection to Untrusted Site ('Open Redirect')
cwes.desc: The web application accepts a user-controlled input that specifies a link to an external site, and uses that link in a redirect.
cwes.description: Unvalidated redirect/forward; user directed to malicious site.

cwes.name: Improper Restriction of XML External Entity Reference
cwes.desc: The product processes an XML document that can contain XML entities with URIs that resolve to documents outside of the intended sphere of control, causing the product to embed incorrect documents into

cwes.name: Improper Authorization of Index Containing Sensitive Information
cwes.desc: The product creates a search index of private or sensitive documents, but it does not properly limit index access to actors who are authorized to see the original information.

cwes.name: Insufficient Session Expiration
cwes.desc: According to WASC, Insufficient Session Expiration is when a web site permits an attacker to reuse old session credentials or session IDs for authorization.

cwes.name: Sensitive Cookie in HTTPS Session Without 'Secure' Attribute
cwes.desc: The Secure attribute for sensitive cookies in HTTPS sessions is not set.

cwes.name: Exposed Unsafe ActiveX Method
cwes.desc: An ActiveX control is intended for use in a web browser, but it exposes dangerous methods that perform actions that are outside of the browser's security model (e.g. the zone or domain).

cwes.name: Unsafe ActiveX Control Marked Safe For Scripting
cwes.desc: An ActiveX control is intended for restricted use, but it has been marked as safe-for-scripting.

cwes.name: External Control of Critical State Data
cwes.desc: The product stores security-critical state information about its users, or the product itself, in a location that is accessible to unauthorized actors.

cwes.name: Improper Neutralization of HTTP Headers for Scripting Syntax
cwes.desc: The product does not neutralize or incorrectly neutralizes web scripting syntax in HTTP headers that can be used by web browser components that can process raw headers, such as Flash.

cwes.desc: The product defines policy namespaces and makes authorization decisions based on the assumption that a URL is canonical. This can allow a non-canonical URL to bypass the authorization.

cwes.name: Incomplete Denylist to Cross-Site Scripting
cwes.desc: The product uses a denylist-based protection mechanism to defend against XSS attacks, but the denylist is incomplete, allowing XSS variants to succeed.

cwes.name: Incorrect Behavior Order
cwes.desc: The product performs multiple related behaviors, but the behaviors are performed in the wrong order in ways that may produce resultant weaknesses.

cwes.name: Execution After Redirect (EAR)
cwes.desc: The web application sends a redirect to another location, but instead of exiting, it executes additional code.

cwes.name: Missing Custom Error Page
cwes.desc: The product does not return custom error pages to the user, possibly exposing sensitive information.

cwes.desc: The product uses a protection mechanism that relies on the existence or values of a cookie, but it does not properly ensure that the cookie is valid for the associated user.

cwes.name: Improper Control of Interaction Frequency
cwes.desc: The product does not properly limit the number or frequency of interactions that it has with an actor, such as the number of incoming requests.

threats.desc: In-vehicle IVI embedded browser (Tesla QtWebKit 534.34, BMW NBT browser) renders malicious web content delivered via Wi-Fi hotspot or cellular; WebKit UAF/OOB Write gives control of the renderer process → IVI OS privilege escalation → CAN bus access. Tesla FREE-FALL (2017): Wi-Fi → QtWebKit (CVE-2016-4657) → Ubuntu root.

threats.name: Browser Sandbox Escape via IVI Platform Privilege
threats.desc: Vehicle IVI browsers lack the sandbox present in desktop browsers; after achieving renderer RCE, the attacker can directly access D-Bus, the filesystem, and CAN interfaces at the OS level (Ubuntu/QNX) without a separate privilege escalation step.

Browser Engine (WebKit/QtWebKit)._note: Embedded browser engine in the vehicle IVI system. Tesla Model S/X CID: QtWebKit 534.34 (FREE-FALL 2017, CVE-2016-4657). Tesla OTA 2018: QtCarBrowser. BMW NBT: ConnectedDrive browser component. PID-BROW1 threat: remote web content → browser RCE → IVI compromise.

cwes.name: Improper Control of Filename for Include/Require Statement in PHP Program ('PHP Remote File Inclusion')
cwes.desc: The PHP application receives input from an upstream component, but it does not restrict or incorrectly restricts the input before its usage in require, include, or similar functions.

cwes.name: Improper Neutralization of CRLF Sequences in HTTP Headers ('HTTP Request/Response Splitting')
cwes.desc: The product receives data from an HTTP agent/component (e.g., web server, proxy, browser, etc.), but it does not neutralize or incorrectly neutralizes CR and LF characters before the data is included

cwes.name: Improper Encoding or Escaping of Output
cwes.desc: The product prepares a structured message for communication with another component, but encoding or escaping of the data is either missing or done incorrectly. As a result, the intended structure of t

cwes.name: Exposure of Sensitive Information to an Unauthorized Actor
cwes.desc: The product exposes sensitive information to an actor that is not explicitly authorized to have access to that information.
cwes.description: Sensitive information exposed to unauthorized actors via error messages or logs.

cwes.name: Storage of File with Sensitive Data Under Web Root
cwes.desc: The product stores sensitive data under the web document root with insufficient access control, which might make it accessible to untrusted parties.

cwes.name: J2EE Bad Practices: Direct Management of Connections
cwes.desc: The J2EE application directly manages connections, instead of using the container's connection management facilities.

cwes.name: Improper Access Control
cwes.desc: The product does not restrict or incorrectly restricts access to a resource from an unauthorized actor.
cwes.description: Access control mechanisms not properly enforced; unauthorized actions possible.

cwes.name: Improper Authorization
cwes.desc: The product does not perform or incorrectly performs an authorization check when an actor attempts to access a resource or perform an action.
cwes.description: Authorization check absent or incorrectly implemented.

cwes.name: Improper Authentication
cwes.desc: When an actor claims to have a given identity, the product does not prove or insufficiently proves that the claim is correct.
cwes.description: Authentication mechanisms are weak, missing, or bypassable.

cwes.name: Authentication Bypass Using an Alternate Path or Channel
cwes.desc: The product requires authentication, but the product has an alternate path or channel that does not require authentication.

cwes.name: Improper Certificate Validation
cwes.desc: The product does not validate, or incorrectly validates, a certificate.
cwes.description: TLS certificate not properly validated; MitM attack possible.

cwes.name: Improper Following of a Certificate's Chain of Trust
cwes.desc: The product does not follow, or incorrectly follows, the chain of trust for a certificate back to a trusted root certificate, resulting in incorrect trust of any resource that is associated with that

cwes.name: Improper Validation of Certificate with Host Mismatch
cwes.desc: The product communicates with a host that provides a certificate, but the product does not properly ensure that the certificate is actually associated with that host.

cwes.name: Improper Check for Certificate Revocation
cwes.desc: The product does not check or incorrectly checks the revocation status of a certificate, which may cause it to use a certificate that has been compromised.

cwes.name: Authentication Bypass by Assumed-Immutable Data
cwes.desc: The authentication scheme or implementation uses key data elements that are assumed to be immutable, but can be controlled or modified by the attacker.

cwes.name: Origin Validation Error
cwes.desc: The product does not properly verify that the source of data or communication is valid.
cwes.description: Message origin not validated; forged messages accepted as legitimate.

cwes.name: Cross-Site Request Forgery (CSRF)
cwes.desc: The web application does not, or cannot, sufficiently verify whether a request was intentionally provided by the user who sent the request, which could have originated from an unauthorized actor.

cwes.name: J2EE Bad Practices: Use of System.exit()
cwes.desc: A J2EE application uses System.exit(), which also shuts down its container.

cwes.name: J2EE Bad Practices: Direct Use of Threads
cwes.desc: Thread management in a Web application is forbidden in some circumstances and is always highly error prone.

cwes.name: Session Fixation
cwes.desc: Authenticating a user, or otherwise establishing a new user session, without invalidating any existing session identifier gives an attacker the opportunity to steal authenticated sessions.

cwes.name: Improper Protection of Alternate Path
cwes.desc: The product does not sufficiently protect all possible paths that a user can take to access restricted functionality or resources.

cwes.name: Direct Request ('Forced Browsing')
cwes.desc: The web application does not adequately enforce appropriate authorization on all restricted URLs, scripts, or files.

cwes.name: Unparsed Raw Web Content Delivery
cwes.desc: The product stores raw content or supporting code under the web document root with an extension that is not specifically handled by the server.

cwes.name: Unrestricted Upload of File with Dangerous Type
cwes.desc: The product allows the upload or transfer of dangerous file types that are automatically processed within its environment.

cwes.name: Inconsistent Interpretation of HTTP Requests ('HTTP Request/Response Smuggling')
cwes.desc: The product acts as an intermediary HTTP agent (such as a proxy or firewall) in the data flow between two entities such as a client and server, but it does not interpret malformed HTTP requests or res

cwes.name: Modification of Assumed-Immutable Data (MAID)
cwes.desc: The product does not properly protect an assumed-immutable element from being modified by an attacker.

cwes.name: External Control of Assumed-Immutable Web Parameter
cwes.desc: The web application does not sufficiently verify inputs that are assumed to be immutable but are actually externally controllable, such as hidden form fields.

cwes.name: Exposure of Sensitive System Information to an Unauthorized Control Sphere
cwes.desc: The product does not properly prevent sensitive system-level information from being accessed by unauthorized actors who do not have the same level of access to the underlying system as the product doe

cwes.name: .NET Misconfiguration: Use of Impersonation
cwes.desc: Allowing a .NET application to run at potentially escalated levels of access to the underlying operating and file systems can be dangerous and result in various forms of attacks.

cwes.name: Insufficiently Protected Credentials
cwes.desc: The product transmits or stores authentication credentials, but it uses an insecure method that is susceptible to unauthorized interception and/or retrieval.

cwes.name: Use of Web Browser Cache Containing Sensitive Information
cwes.desc: The web application does not use an appropriate caching policy that specifies the extent to which each web page and associated form fields should be cached.

cwes.name: Exposure of Version-Control Repository to an Unauthorized Control Sphere
cwes.desc: The product stores a CVS, git, or other repository in a directory, archive, or other resource that is stored, transferred, or otherwise made accessible to unauthorized actors.

cwes.name: Exposure of Backup File to an Unauthorized Control Sphere
cwes.desc: A backup file is stored in a directory or archive that is made accessible to unauthorized actors.

cwes.name: Servlet Runtime Error Message Containing Sensitive Information
cwes.desc: A servlet error message indicates that there exists an unhandled exception in the web application code and may provide useful information to an attacker.

cwes.name: Use of Persistent Cookies Containing Sensitive Information
cwes.desc: The web application uses persistent cookies, but the cookies contain sensitive information.

cwes.name: Inclusion of Sensitive Information in Source Code
cwes.desc: Source code on a web server or repository often contains sensitive information and should generally not be accessible to users.

cwes.name: Missing Password Field Masking
cwes.desc: The product does not mask passwords during entry, increasing the potential for attackers to observe and capture passwords.

cwes.name: Incorrect Behavior Order: Authorization Before Parsing and Canonicalization
cwes.desc: If a web server does not fully parse requested URLs before it examines them for authorization, it may be possible for an attacker to bypass authorization protection.

cwes.name: Command Shell in Externally Accessible Directory
cwes.desc: A possible shell file exists in /cgi-bin/ or other accessible directories. This is extremely dangerous and can be used by an attacker to execute commands on the web server.

cwes.name: J2EE Framework: Saving Unserializable Objects to Disk
cwes.desc: When the J2EE container attempts to write unserializable objects to disk there is no guarantee that the process will complete successfully.

cwes.name: Use of GET Request Method With Sensitive Query Strings
cwes.desc: The web application uses the HTTP GET method to process a request and includes sensitive information in the query string of that request.

cwes.name: Uncaught Exception in Servlet
cwes.desc: The Servlet does not catch all exceptions, which may reveal sensitive debugging information.

cwes.name: URL Redirection to Untrusted Site ('Open Redirect')
cwes.desc: The web application accepts a user-controlled input that specifies a link to an external site, and uses that link in a redirect.
cwes.description: Unvalidated redirect/forward; user directed to malicious site.

cwes.name: Improper Restriction of XML External Entity Reference
cwes.desc: The product processes an XML document that can contain XML entities with URIs that resolve to documents outside of the intended sphere of control, causing the product to embed incorrect documents into

cwes.name: Improper Authorization of Index Containing Sensitive Information
cwes.desc: The product creates a search index of private or sensitive documents, but it does not properly limit index access to actors who are authorized to see the original information.

cwes.name: Insufficient Session Expiration
cwes.desc: According to WASC, Insufficient Session Expiration is when a web site permits an attacker to reuse old session credentials or session IDs for authorization.

cwes.name: Sensitive Cookie in HTTPS Session Without 'Secure' Attribute
cwes.desc: The Secure attribute for sensitive cookies in HTTPS sessions is not set.

cwes.name: Exposed Unsafe ActiveX Method
cwes.desc: An ActiveX control is intended for use in a web browser, but it exposes dangerous methods that perform actions that are outside of the browser's security model (e.g. the zone or domain).

cwes.name: Unsafe ActiveX Control Marked Safe For Scripting
cwes.desc: An ActiveX control is intended for restricted use, but it has been marked as safe-for-scripting.

cwes.name: External Control of Critical State Data
cwes.desc: The product stores security-critical state information about its users, or the product itself, in a location that is accessible to unauthorized actors.

cwes.name: Improper Neutralization of HTTP Headers for Scripting Syntax
cwes.desc: The product does not neutralize or incorrectly neutralizes web scripting syntax in HTTP headers that can be used by web browser components that can process raw headers, such as Flash.

cwes.name: Reliance on File Name or Extension of Externally-Supplied File
cwes.desc: The product allows a file to be uploaded, but it relies on the file name or extension of the file to determine the appropriate behaviors. This could be used by attackers to cause the file to be miscla

cwes.name: Use of Non-Canonical URL Paths for Authorization Decisions
cwes.desc: The product defines policy namespaces and makes authorization decisions based on the assumption that a URL is canonical. This can allow a non-canonical URL to bypass the authorization.

cwes.name: Trusting HTTP Permission Methods on the Server Side
cwes.desc: The server contains a protection mechanism that assumes that any URI that is accessed using HTTP GET will not cause a state change to the associated resource. This might allow attackers to bypass inte

cwes.name: Exposure of WSDL File Containing Sensitive Information
cwes.desc: The Web services architecture may require exposing a Web Service Definition Language (WSDL) file that contains information on the publicly accessible services and how callers of these services should

cwes.name: Incomplete Denylist to Cross-Site Scripting
cwes.desc: The product uses a denylist-based protection mechanism to defend against XSS attacks, but the denylist is incomplete, allowing XSS variants to succeed.

cwes.name: Incorrect Behavior Order
cwes.desc: The product performs multiple related behaviors, but the behaviors are performed in the wrong order in ways that may produce resultant weaknesses.

cwes.name: Execution After Redirect (EAR)
cwes.desc: The web application sends a redirect to another location, but instead of exiting, it executes additional code.

cwes.name: Missing Custom Error Page
cwes.desc: The product does not return custom error pages to the user, possibly exposing sensitive information.

cwes.name: Reliance on Cookies without Validation and Integrity Checking in a Security Decision
cwes.desc: The product uses a protection mechanism that relies on the existence or values of a cookie, but it does not properly ensure that the cookie is valid for the associated user.

cwes.name: Improper Control of Interaction Frequency
cwes.desc: The product does not properly limit the number or frequency of interactions that it has with an actor, such as the number of incoming requests.

cwes.name: Guessable CAPTCHA
cwes.desc: The product uses a CAPTCHA challenge, but the challenge can be guessed or automatically recognized by a non-human actor.

cwes.name: Reliance on Untrusted Inputs in a Security Decision
cwes.desc: The product uses a protection mechanism that relies on the existence or values of an input, but the input can be modified by an untrusted actor in a way that bypasses the protection mechanism.

cwes.name: Inclusion of Web Functionality from an Untrusted Source
cwes.desc: The product includes web functionality (such as a web widget) from another domain, which causes it to operate within the domain of the product, potentially granting total access and control of the pro

cwes.name: Improper Enforcement of a Single, Unique Action
cwes.desc: The product requires that an actor should only be able to perform an action once, or to have only one unique action, but the product does not enforce or improperly enforces this restriction.

cwes.name: Missing Authorization
cwes.desc: The product does not perform an authorization check when an actor attempts to access a resource or perform an action.
cwes.description: Authorization check missing; operation proceeds without verification.

cwes.name: Incorrect Authorization
cwes.desc: The product performs an authorization check when an actor attempts to access a resource or perform an action, but it does not correctly perform the check.

cwes.name: Server-Side Request Forgery (SSRF)
cwes.desc: The web server receives a URL or similar request from an upstream component and retrieves the contents of this URL, but it does not sufficiently ensure that the request is being sent to the expected d

cwes.name: Improper Restriction of Communication Channel to Intended Endpoints
cwes.desc: The product establishes a communication channel to (or from) an endpoint for privileged or protected operations, but it does not properly ensure that it is communicating with the correct endpoint.

cwes.name: Permissive Cross-domain Security Policy with Untrusted Domains
cwes.desc: The product uses a web-client protection mechanism such as a Content Security Policy (CSP) or cross-domain policy file, but the policy includes untrusted domains with which the web client is allowed t

cwes.name: Sensitive Cookie Without 'HttpOnly' Flag
cwes.desc: The product uses a cookie to store sensitive information, but the cookie is not marked with the HttpOnly flag.

cwes.name: Insufficient Visual Distinction of Homoglyphs Presented to User
cwes.desc: The product displays information or identifiers to a user, but the display mechanism does not make it easy for the user to distinguish between visually similar or identical glyphs (homoglyphs), which

cwes.name: Improper Restriction of Rendered UI Layers or Frames
cwes.desc: The web application does not restrict or incorrectly restricts frame objects or UI layers that belong to another application or domain, which can lead to user confusion about which interface the user

cwes.name: Use of Web Link to Untrusted Target with window.opener Access
cwes.desc: The web application produces links to untrusted external sites outside of its sphere of control, but it does not properly prevent the external site from modifying security-critical properties of the w

cwes.name: Application-Level Admin Tool with Inconsistent View of Underlying Operating System
cwes.desc: The product provides an application for administrators to manage parts of the underlying operating system, but the application does not accurately identify all of the relevant entities or resources th

cwes.name: Sensitive Cookie with Improper SameSite Attribute
cwes.desc: The SameSite attribute for sensitive cookies is not set, or an insecure value is used.

cwes.name: Missing Origin Validation in WebSockets
cwes.desc: The product uses a WebSocket, but it does not properly verify that the source of data or communication is valid.

cwes.desc: The product does not neutralize or incorrectly neutralizes user-controllable input before it is placed in output that is used as a web page that is served to other users.
cwes.description: XSS: Attacker injects scripts that execute in victim's browser context.

cwes.name: Improper Neutralization of Directives in Dynamically Evaluated Code ('Eval Injection')
cwes.desc: The product receives input from an upstream component, but it does not neutralize or incorrectly neutralizes code syntax before using the input in a dynamic evaluation call (e.g. eval).

cwes.desc: The product deserializes untrusted data without sufficiently ensuring that the resulting data will be valid.

cwes.desc: The product uses a template engine to insert or process externally-influenced input, but it does not neutralize or incorrectly neutralizes special elements or syntax that can be interpreted as templat

cwes.name: Improper Validation of Generative AI Output

cwes.name: Improper Neutralization of Input Used for LLM Prompting

cwes.name: Insecure Setting of Generative AI/ML Model Inference Parameters
cwes.desc: The product has a component that relies on a generative AI/ML model configured with inference parameters that produce an unacceptably high rate of erroneous or unexpected outputs.

cwes.name: J2EE Misconfiguration: Insufficient Session-ID Length
cwes.desc: The J2EE application is configured to use an insufficient session ID length.

cwes.name: J2EE Misconfiguration: Missing Custom Error Page
cwes.desc: The default error page of a web application should not display sensitive information about the product.

cwes.name: Improper Neutralization of Script in Attributes of IMG Tags in a Web Page
cwes.desc: The web application does not neutralize or incorrectly neutralizes scripting elements within attributes of HTML IMG tags, such as the src attribute.

cwes.name: Improper Neutralization of Script in Attributes in a Web Page
cwes.desc: The product does not neutralize or incorrectly neutralizes javascript: or other URIs from dangerous attributes within tags, such as onmouseover, onload, onerror, or style.

cwes.name: Improper Neutralization of Encoded URI Schemes in a Web Page
cwes.desc: The web application improperly neutralizes user-controlled input for executable script disguised with URI encodings.

cwes.name: Doubled Character XSS Manipulations
cwes.desc: The web application does not filter user-controlled input for executable script disguised using doubling of the involved characters.

cwes.name: Improper Neutralization of Invalid Characters in Identifiers in Web Pages
cwes.desc: The product does not neutralize or incorrectly neutralizes invalid characters or byte sequences in the middle of tag names, URI schemes, and other identifiers.

cwes.name: Improper Neutralization of Server-Side Includes (SSI) Within a Web Page
cwes.desc: The product generates a web page, but does not neutralize or incorrectly neutralizes user-controllable input that could be interpreted as a server-side include (SSI) directive.

cwes.desc: A servlet error message indicates that there exists an unhandled exception in the web application code and may provide useful information to an attacker.

cwes.name: Use of Persistent Cookies Containing Sensitive Information
cwes.desc: The web application uses persistent cookies, but the cookies contain sensitive information.

cwes.name: Inclusion of Sensitive Information in Source Code
cwes.desc: Source code on a web server or repository often contains sensitive information and should generally not be accessible to users.

cwes.name: Missing Password Field Masking
cwes.desc: The product does not mask passwords during entry, increasing the potential for attackers to observe and capture passwords.

cwes.name: Incorrect Behavior Order: Authorization Before Parsing and Canonicalization
cwes.desc: If a web server does not fully parse requested URLs before it examines them for authorization, it may be possible for an attacker to bypass authorization protection.

cwes.name: Command Shell in Externally Accessible Directory
cwes.desc: A possible shell file exists in /cgi-bin/ or other accessible directories. This is extremely dangerous and can be used by an attacker to execute commands on the web server.

cwes.name: J2EE Framework: Saving Unserializable Objects to Disk
cwes.desc: When the J2EE container attempts to write unserializable objects to disk there is no guarantee that the process will complete successfully.

cwes.name: Use of GET Request Method With Sensitive Query Strings
cwes.desc: The web application uses the HTTP GET method to process a request and includes sensitive information in the query string of that request.

cwes.name: Uncaught Exception in Servlet
cwes.desc: The Servlet does not catch all exceptions, which may reveal sensitive debugging information.

cwes.name: URL Redirection to Untrusted Site ('Open Redirect')
cwes.desc: The web application accepts a user-controlled input that specifies a link to an external site, and uses that link in a redirect.
cwes.description: Unvalidated redirect/forward; user directed to malicious site.

cwes.name: Improper Restriction of XML External Entity Reference
cwes.desc: The product processes an XML document that can contain XML entities with URIs that resolve to documents outside of the intended sphere of control, causing the product to embed incorrect documents into

cwes.name: Improper Authorization of Index Containing Sensitive Information
cwes.desc: The product creates a search index of private or sensitive documents, but it does not properly limit index access to actors who are authorized to see the original information.

cwes.name: Insufficient Session Expiration
cwes.desc: According to WASC, Insufficient Session Expiration is when a web site permits an attacker to reuse old session credentials or session IDs for authorization.

cwes.name: Sensitive Cookie in HTTPS Session Without 'Secure' Attribute
cwes.desc: The Secure attribute for sensitive cookies in HTTPS sessions is not set.

cwes.name: Exposed Unsafe ActiveX Method
cwes.desc: An ActiveX control is intended for use in a web browser, but it exposes dangerous methods that perform actions that are outside of the browser's security model (e.g. the zone or domain).

cwes.name: Unsafe ActiveX Control Marked Safe For Scripting
cwes.desc: An ActiveX control is intended for restricted use, but it has been marked as safe-for-scripting.

cwes.name: External Control of Critical State Data
cwes.desc: The product stores security-critical state information about its users, or the product itself, in a location that is accessible to unauthorized actors.

cwes.name: Improper Neutralization of HTTP Headers for Scripting Syntax
cwes.desc: The product does not neutralize or incorrectly neutralizes web scripting syntax in HTTP headers that can be used by web browser components that can process raw headers, such as Flash.

cwes.name: Reliance on File Name or Extension of Externally-Supplied File
cwes.desc: The product allows a file to be uploaded, but it relies on the file name or extension of the file to determine the appropriate behaviors. This could be used by attackers to cause the file to be miscla

cwes.name: Use of Non-Canonical URL Paths for Authorization Decisions
cwes.desc: The product defines policy namespaces and makes authorization decisions based on the assumption that a URL is canonical. This can allow a non-canonical URL to bypass the authorization.

cwes.name: Trusting HTTP Permission Methods on the Server Side
cwes.desc: The server contains a protection mechanism that assumes that any URI that is accessed using HTTP GET will not cause a state change to the associated resource. This might allow attackers to bypass inte

cwes.name: Exposure of WSDL File Containing Sensitive Information
cwes.desc: The Web services architecture may require exposing a Web Service Definition Language (WSDL) file that contains information on the publicly accessible services and how callers of these services should

cwes.name: Incomplete Denylist to Cross-Site Scripting
cwes.desc: The product uses a denylist-based protection mechanism to defend against XSS attacks, but the denylist is incomplete, allowing XSS variants to succeed.

cwes.name: Incorrect Behavior Order
cwes.desc: The product performs multiple related behaviors, but the behaviors are performed in the wrong order in ways that may produce resultant weaknesses.

cwes.name: Execution After Redirect (EAR)
cwes.desc: The web application sends a redirect to another location, but instead of exiting, it executes additional code.

cwes.name: Missing Custom Error Page
cwes.desc: The product does not return custom error pages to the user, possibly exposing sensitive information.

cwes.name: Reliance on Cookies without Validation and Integrity Checking in a Security Decision
cwes.desc: The product uses a protection mechanism that relies on the existence or values of a cookie, but it does not properly ensure that the cookie is valid for the associated user.

cwes.name: Improper Control of Interaction Frequency
cwes.desc: The product does not properly limit the number or frequency of interactions that it has with an actor, such as the number of incoming requests.

cwes.name: Guessable CAPTCHA
cwes.desc: The product uses a CAPTCHA challenge, but the challenge can be guessed or automatically recognized by a non-human actor.

cwes.name: Reliance on Untrusted Inputs in a Security Decision
cwes.desc: The product uses a protection mechanism that relies on the existence or values of an input, but the input can be modified by an untrusted actor in a way that bypasses the protection mechanism.

cwes.name: Inclusion of Web Functionality from an Untrusted Source
cwes.desc: The product includes web functionality (such as a web widget) from another domain, which causes it to operate within the domain of the product, potentially granting total access and control of the pro

cwes.name: Improper Enforcement of a Single, Unique Action
cwes.desc: The product requires that an actor should only be able to perform an action once, or to have only one unique action, but the product does not enforce or improperly enforces this restriction.

cwes.name: Incorrect Authorization
cwes.desc: The product performs an authorization check when an actor attempts to access a resource or perform an action, but it does not correctly perform the check.

cwes.name: Improper Restriction of Communication Channel to Intended Endpoints
cwes.desc: The product establishes a communication channel to (or from) an endpoint for privileged or protected operations, but it does not properly ensure that it is communicating with the correct endpoint.

cwes.name: Permissive Cross-domain Security Policy with Untrusted Domains
cwes.desc: The product uses a web-client protection mechanism such as a Content Security Policy (CSP) or cross-domain policy file, but the policy includes untrusted domains with which the web client is allowed t

cwes.name: Sensitive Cookie Without 'HttpOnly' Flag
cwes.desc: The product uses a cookie to store sensitive information, but the cookie is not marked with the HttpOnly flag.

cwes.name: Insufficient Visual Distinction of Homoglyphs Presented to User
cwes.desc: The product displays information or identifiers to a user, but the display mechanism does not make it easy for the user to distinguish between visually similar or identical glyphs (homoglyphs), which

cwes.name: Improper Restriction of Rendered UI Layers or Frames
cwes.desc: The web application does not restrict or incorrectly restricts frame objects or UI layers that belong to another application or domain, which can lead to user confusion about which interface the user

cwes.name: Use of Web Link to Untrusted Target with window.opener Access
cwes.desc: The web application produces links to untrusted external sites outside of its sphere of control, but it does not properly prevent the external site from modifying security-critical properties of the w

cwes.name: Application-Level Admin Tool with Inconsistent View of Underlying Operating System
cwes.desc: The product provides an application for administrators to manage parts of the underlying operating system, but the application does not accurately identify all of the relevant entities or resources th

cwes.name: Sensitive Cookie with Improper SameSite Attribute
cwes.desc: The SameSite attribute for sensitive cookies is not set, or an insecure value is used.

cwes.name: Binding to an Unrestricted IP Address
cwes.desc: The product assigns the address 0.0.0.0 for a database server, a cloud service/instance, or any computing resource that communicates remotely.

cwes.name: Missing Origin Validation in WebSockets
cwes.desc: The product uses a WebSocket, but it does not properly verify that the source of data or communication is valid.

cwes.name: Improper Neutralization of Special Elements used in an SQL Command ('SQL Injection')
