UDS (Unified Diagnostic Services)._note: ISO 14229 Unified Diagnostic Services, running over CAN or In-Vehicle Ethernet. Key services: SecurityAccess (0x27), ECUReset (0x11), WriteDataByIdentifier (0x2E). Bypassing Seed-Key allows arbitrary ECU reflashing. All 5 test cases include UDS-based attack steps.

cwes.name: Improper Access Control
cwes.description: OBD-II port provides unauthenticated access to vehicle CAN bus.

cwes.name: Missing Authentication for Critical Function
cwes.description: Physical OBD-II access allows direct ECU communication without any credential.

cwes.name: Unprotected Primary Channel
cwes.description: OBD-II serves as unprotected entry point to in-vehicle CAN network.

PID-OBD1.name: Vehicle exposes OBD-II (SAE J1979) diagnostic port

threats.description: Physical OBD-II port provides unauthenticated CAN bus access; malicious OBD dongle can inject frames.
OBD-II Bluetooth/Wi-Fi dongle widens attack surface to wireless; compromised dongle bridges CAN to remote attacker.

OBD-II (SAE J1979)._note: SAE J1979 / ISO 15031 on-board diagnostic port (16-pin connector). Mandatory on all US-sold vehicles since 1996. Physical access grants unauthenticated direct access to the CAN bus, enabling ECU diagnostics and manipulation. Also exploited as a wireless attack path via OBD-II Wi-Fi/BLE dongles. Test case: Jeep 2015 — physical OBD-II access used to inject CAN frames directly.

cwes.name: Improper Certificate Validation
cwes.desc: Missing HTTPS server certificate validation enables MITM; attacker impersonates the OTA update server to distribute malicious firmware.
cwes.description: TLS certificate not properly validated; MitM attack possible.

cves.description: Heartbleed: OpenSSL 1.0.1a–1.0.1f TLS heartbeat extension buffer over-read. Leaks up to 64KB server/client memory including private keys. BMW 2019: ConnectedDrive backend and TCU TLS stack used OpenSSL; Mercedes 2020: backend API servers used OpenSSL; Tesla OTA HTTPS channel.
Erlang/OTP TLS server accepts wildcard certificate without proper validation. MITM on TLS connections. Affects embedded OTP/MQTT brokers in telematics stacks.

cwes.name: Cleartext Transmission of Sensitive Information
cwes.desc: Vehicle control commands or location data transmitted over plain HTTP (no encryption) → susceptible to sniffing.
cwes.description: Sensitive data sent in cleartext over unencrypted channel.

cves.description: POODLE: TLS 1.0 downgrade to SSLv3 padding oracle. Decrypts session cookies via CBC padding oracle with ~256 requests. Automotive backend systems with SSLv3 fallback (pre-2015) affected.

cwes.name: URL Redirection to Untrusted Site ('Open Redirect')
cwes.desc: Unvalidated URL redirect in the in-vehicle browser or OTA client; attacker redirects to a malicious server delivering malicious content.
cwes.description: Unvalidated redirect/forward; user directed to malicious site.

cwes.name: Cross-Site Request Forgery (CSRF)
cwes.desc: CSRF vulnerability in an in-vehicle HTTP service (management web interface); malicious web page changes vehicle configuration.

PID-HTTP1: Vehicle backend or OTA uses H.name: Vehicle communicates with remote backend or OTA server via HTTP or HTTPS

threats.name: MITM Attack on Vehicle-Backend HTTP/HTTPS Channel
threats.desc: BMW TCB HTTP channel (ConnectedDrive API), Tesla OTA HTTPS, and Mercedes HERMES HTTPS key exchange channel are vulnerable to MITM; missing certificate validation or certificate pinning allows traffic redirection to an attacker server → malicious OTA payload or command injection.

PID-411: Remote services with sensitive.name: Device exposes remote services with the ability to send, receive, view or modify sensitive information or configurations

HTTP / HTTPS._note: HTTP/HTTPS used for vehicle-to-backend OTA updates, remote diagnostics, and telematics API calls. Missing certificate validation (CWE-295) or cleartext transmission (CWE-319) enables man-in-the-middle attacks. Applicable to Tesla OTA, BMW NGTP HTTP wrapper, and Mercedes backend API.

cwes.name: Missing Encryption of Sensitive Data
cwes.desc: Sensitive vehicle data in NGTP message fields transmitted without encryption.
cwes.description: Sensitive data transmitted or stored without encryption.

cwes.name: Insufficient Verification of Data Authenticity
cwes.desc: Insufficient sender authentication on NGTP vehicle command messages enables command forgery.
cwes.description: Data authenticity not sufficiently verified; spoofed messages accepted.

cves.description: BMW ConnectedDrive NGTP command authentication vulnerability. Insufficient message signing allows crafted NGTP commands to remotely unlock doors, disable alarms, trigger remote start. Disclosed by Keen Security Lab in BMW security research report (January 2019). Affects BMW TCB (Telematics Control Unit) with NGTP over cellular.

cwes.name: Authentication Bypass by Capture-replay
cwes.desc: Absent replay prevention (nonce, timestamp) in NGTP sessions allows captured legitimate commands to be replayed.
cwes.description: Authentication protocol vulnerable to replay attack.

cwes.name: Use of a Broken or Risky Cryptographic Algorithm
cwes.desc: Weak algorithm or short key used for NGTP encryption.

PID-NGTP1: BMW TCB uses NGTP for telemat.name: Device implements NGTP (Next Generation Telematics Protocol) for receiving vehicle commands

threats.name: Unauthorized Vehicle Command via NGTP Authentication Bypass
threats.desc: BMW ConnectedDrive NGTP command channel authentication vulnerability; a forged server or NGTP command forgery enables unauthorized remote vehicle control (door unlock, window control, service activation). BMW 2019 research (Cai et al.) identified NGTP stack involvement in the TCB attack chain.

cwes.name: Insufficient Verification of Data Authenticity
cwes.desc: NGTP command sender is not sufficiently authenticated.

cwes.name: Authentication Bypass by Capture-replay
cwes.desc: NGTP lacks replay protection allowing message replay.

cwes.name: Missing Encryption of Sensitive Data
cwes.desc: NGTP fields not encrypted, enabling eavesdropping.

cwes.name: Insufficient Verification of Data Authenticity
cwes.desc: NGTP command sender is not sufficiently authenticated.

cwes.desc: NGTP lacks replay protection allowing message replay.

cwes.name: Missing Encryption of Sensitive Data
cwes.desc: NGTP fields not encrypted, enabling eavesdropping.

NGTP (Telematics Protocol)._note: NGTP (Next Generation Telematics Protocol) used by the BMW TCB (Telematics Control Unit). Delivers remote lock/unlock, eCall, and vehicle tracking commands via encrypted SMS/TCP. Absent command signature verification allows remote control command forgery (BMW 2019 test case, CVE-2019-9493).

cwes.name: Cleartext Transmission of Sensitive Information
cwes.description: MQTT without TLS transmits telemetry data in plaintext.

cwes.name: Improper Authentication
cwes.description: MQTT broker with no/weak authentication allows unauthorized publish/subscribe.

cwes.name: Uncontrolled Resource Consumption
cwes.description: Unauthenticated MQTT publish floods broker, causing denial of service.

cwes.name: Missing Authentication for Critical Function
cwes.description: CoAP endpoints without DTLS allow unauthenticated resource manipulation.

PID-MQTT1.name: Device uses MQTT or CoAP for IoT telemetry or V2X messaging

threats.description: MQTT broker without mTLS: attacker subscribes to vehicle telemetry topics (location, speed).
Malicious MQTT PUBLISH floods broker causing DoS for legitimate vehicle-to-cloud messages.

MQTT / CoAP._note: MQTT (Message Queuing Telemetry Transport, ISO/IEC 20922) and CoAP (Constrained Application Protocol, RFC 7252). Lightweight IoT/V2X telematics messaging protocols. Without TLS/DTLS: cleartext transmission; without mTLS: unauthorized publish/subscribe. Growing attack surface in future connected-vehicle V2X infrastructure.
